scene_vendor_argument_open: A ring of shoulders closes around two vendors arguing over the same corner. Nobody's leaving.
The halal cart and the bodega extension table are three feet apart. Both owners are explaining the law.
A folding table goes over. Produce rolls into the gutter. People watch like it's pay-per-view.
Two vendors are nose-to-nose outside the scaffolding. At least six people have their phones up.
A crowd stops moving on {neighborhood}. Someone's cart is in someone else's spot. This is a formal dispute now.
scene_vendor_argument_mid: The argument found a referee. The referee is also a vendor.
Someone's grandmother arrived. The situation may or may not improve.
A cop walks past without stopping, which everyone notes.
The tally of wrongs goes back to 2031. Both sides have documentation.
scene_vendor_argument_close: The crowd disperses. The carts are six inches further apart than before. Progress.
Someone paid someone. Nobody admits what for.
The vendors are shaking hands. The handshake takes about thirty seconds.
Nothing resolved. Everyone goes back to what they were doing. The corner still has two carts.
scene_preacher_open: A preacher found a milk crate on {neighborhood} and is using it. He's got a crowd.
A woman with a bullhorn that doesn't need a bullhorn is working the corner. People slowed.
Someone's preaching from the steps. The crowd isn't moving — they want to see where this goes.
A man in a gold raincoat is delivering the end times to a bus stop. Three people missed their bus.
The sermon started at the crosswalk and spread. Twenty people now, none of them tourists.
scene_preacher_mid: The preacher hits a line that makes someone holler back. That's a good sign for him.
He's naming names now. The names are real. The crowd is bigger.
She paused to let a truck pass, then picked up mid-sentence like it was planned.
The crowd isn't shrinking. Someone brought their kid up to listen.
scene_preacher_close: The milk crate goes back under the arm. The crowd breaks apart like it was never there.
He steps down and immediately sells three pamphlets. The pamphlets are hand-stapled.
She walks away still talking. Half the crowd follows half a block.
The corner is empty. Someone left a shoe, which is confusing.
scene_salvage_open: Someone opened a cellar door and found something worth fighting over. There's a crowd.
A caved-in storefront got pushed in further and now it's a dig site. People are watching.
A pile of salvage from the abandoned lot is getting sorted in public. People are making offers.
The vacant lot on {neighborhood} gave something up. A crowd is managing the find.
scene_salvage_mid: The arguing has a structure now. There's a mediator. He's charging for it.
Someone brought a cart. Someone else brought a tarp. A market is forming.
Inventory is being done out loud. Bids are informal but taken seriously.
scene_salvage_close: The lot is empty again. People are walking away with things under their arms.
Whatever was worth taking is taken. The rest stays in the gutter.
The find was split. Nobody agrees it was fair. It was still split.
scene_league_open: Someone brought a portable screen outside and the sidewalk is watching the {neighborhood} game.
The call was wrong and half the block has decided to explain why to the other half.
The game is on in every window. The argument about the game is in the street.
A table of cards stopped mid-hand because the league score just changed. There are feelings.
Everyone on the corner has an opinion about the ref. They're sharing it at volume.
scene_league_mid: The score changed again. The mood changed with it.
An old head is explaining how the '29 season was different. Nobody disagrees but nobody agrees.
Someone owes someone twenty now. The debt is being remembered loudly.
scene_league_close: The game's over. The losing fans don't leave immediately.
The card table reconvenes. The debt question is still open.
The screen gets unplugged. The sidewalk goes back to sidewalk business.
scene_cult_crowd_open: A speaker for one of the new faiths is working {neighborhood}. The crowd isn't skeptical enough to leave.
Someone set up a table with pamphlets and frequency charts. There's a line.
The congregation spilled sidewalk — forty people listening to doctrine read off a phone.
A man with antenna jewelry is explaining signal theology at the corner. He's good at it.
scene_cult_crowd_mid: The doctrine got to the part about the old grid. People are nodding.
Someone asked a question that landed. The speaker went quiet, then answered.
A pamphlet changes hands. Two people argue the margin notes.
scene_cult_crowd_close: The table folds up. Three people got a number. Unclear whose number.
The crowd disperses by degrees. A few linger to argue among themselves.
The pamphlets are gone. The table stays. Someone will take it by morning.
scene_busker_open: Someone strung two speakers off a fire escape and is playing to {neighborhood}. A crowd formed below.
A trio in the bodega doorway is doing something that requires a hat on the ground. It's working.
A kid with a two-piece kit is drumming on the sidewalk and drawing a crowd.
A guy with a busted keyboard amp and extraordinary confidence is performing outside the check-cashing place.
scene_busker_mid: The hat got heavier. The performer noticed.
Someone's dancing. Nobody looks at them directly but nobody moves away.
A bodega cat climbed into the speaker stack. Everyone agrees it improved the sound.
scene_busker_close: The last note hangs. The crowd claps once and scatters.
The gear goes into a rolling cart. Two songs later and the sidewalk is regular again.
Someone offers a gig, hands over a card, walks away. The musician looks at the card for a long time.
scene_stoop_wake_open: Candles line the stoop on {neighborhood}. Flowers, a photo, two folding chairs that fill up and empty and fill.
A memorial stoop on {neighborhood}: balloons, candles, a basketball jersey in a zip bag. People sitting.
There's a corner shrine set up — the kind that means someone died within the last week. People are coming.
Thirty people on and around a stoop. Someone died here recently. The candles are new.
scene_stoop_wake_mid: Someone plays a song from a phone. Everyone recognizes it. Nobody says anything.
An older woman is holding someone up. It isn't going to help but it isn't supposed to.
A man brings water and paper cups out of the building. Nobody asked. Everyone takes one.
scene_stoop_wake_close: The vigil breaks down by midnight. The candles stay through the week.
People peel off in pairs. The photo stays on the stoop under a bag.
The crowd is gone. The flowers haven't moved. Neither has the jersey.
scene_procession_open: A faith procession is coming down {neighborhood} — candles in paper cups, chanting at half-tempo.
Forty people in the street, moving slow, singing. Traffic waits. Nobody honks.
The congregation walks in two lines down {neighborhood}, candles out, drone hymn on a portable speaker.
A religious procession has the street. Participants in pale cloth, candles in both hands.
scene_procession_mid: The chant is the same line repeated. It's getting to you a little.
The procession slows at a corner. A prayer. Then it moves again.
Someone drops a candle. Three people stop to relight it before it goes dark.
scene_procession_close: The procession turns down a side street and the sound fades in stages.
The street empties by the time they're done. Wax drips on the pavement stay.
The last participant is a child who keeps looking back to see if anyone is watching. Someone is.
scene_memorial_open: The block on {neighborhood} has a fresh shrine — printed photo, candles, flowers taped to a pole.
Someone died on this corner in the last few days. The people who knew him are coming out.
A lamppost on {neighborhood} is dressed in zip ties and plastic flowers. A crowd keeps the street.
There's a memorial forming around a stairwell entrance. The candles have a little wax left.
scene_memorial_mid: An argument starts, quiet, about what happened. It doesn't resolve.
A kid puts a flower down, steps back, looks at it, leaves.
Someone prints a new photo and tapes it over the one the rain got to.
scene_memorial_close: The crowd breaks apart. The candles burn lower. The photo stays.
The vigil ends with the morning. The flowers stay until someone moves them.
By morning it's just candles and a name written in marker on the pavement.
scene_cult_vigil_open: A circle of believers on {neighborhood} with candles and a portable antenna. They're not inviting anyone.
The cult is doing its night thing again — facing the old signal tower, candles at their feet.
A dozen figures in the street at midnight, candlelit, arranged by some system that isn't obvious.
Vigil on {neighborhood} — the antenna-worshippers, the ones who wear signal jewelry. Candles and frequency chants.
scene_cult_vigil_mid: The chant is numbers. Frequencies, maybe, or coordinates. Nobody explains.
The candles all go out at once. They relight them without comment.
A figure in the middle speaks. The others face away to listen.
scene_cult_vigil_close: The circle dissolves without a signal. The candles stay lit until they don't.
They leave in one direction. The street is empty and smells like tallow.
The vigil ends. The antenna gets packed into a rolling bag.
scene_rats_open: The trash pile on {neighborhood} is moving. Rats are boiling out from the bottom.
Someone tipped a dumpster and the rats inside had opinions about it.
The corner pile is alive. Rats peeling out across the sidewalk in all directions.
scene_rats_mid: The tide of rats hasn't finished. More keep coming.
They're heading toward the drain. Most of them.
A rat the size of a hand tool sits on the curb not running. It has done this before.
scene_rats_close: They're back in the walls. The pile looks like it was never touched.
The last rat crosses the street. It takes its time.
The swarm is gone. Three people are comparing bites.
scene_rats_basement_open: Something broke in the basement of the rowhouse on {neighborhood}. The rats are electing to leave.
A grate pops up and the basement beneath exports its whole rat colony at once.
Rats coming up through a cracked cellar door — dozens, ignoring everything.
scene_rats_basement_close: The basement sealed itself back up. The rats found the next building.
A super appears with a broom handle too late. He knows it.
The colony is gone. The damage report will take weeks.
scene_rats_halal_open: The halal cart on {neighborhood} is not open. The rats noticed first.
An abandoned cart became an ecosystem. The ecosystem is migrating.
Something under the cart's grease trap let go. The rats are organized about it.
scene_rats_scaffold_open: The scaffolding on {neighborhood} has been up for six years and the rats inside have opinions.
Something disturbed the scaffolding colony. They're streaming out along the boards.
A work crew hit something in the scaffolding housing. The rats are the something.
scene_drunk_brawl_open: Two people outside the bar are doing the slow-motion thing that ends with someone on the ground.
A disagreement on {neighborhood} made it outside and got physical around the corner.
Two guys in the street, shirts involved, at least one of them is going to fall.
Someone threw a bottle. The bottle missed. The second punch didn't.
scene_brawl_mid: It's not over. One of them won't stay down.
Someone is filming. Several people are explaining this is wrong while filming.
The crowd gave them a circle. Fighting inside a circle is polite.
scene_drunk_brawl_close: One of them sat down in the street. The fight apparently over.
They're shaking hands. One of them is bleeding while shaking hands.
The fight ended when a woman yelled from a third-floor window. First try.
scene_hustle_brawl_open: A deal on {neighborhood} broke down publicly. The breakdown has an audience.
Two hustlers are settling a count dispute with their bodies. The count was wrong.
A handshake went bad. The correction is going worse.
scene_hustle_brawl_close: The matter is settled in a way that will be re-litigated later.
One of them left. The other one picked something off the ground and left too.
The crowd disperses fast. Everyone has somewhere to be.
scene_crew_brawl_open: Two crews have a problem at the corner of {neighborhood} that's being solved physically.
This is a territorial conversation. It's gotten to the part without words.
Four people on the sidewalk working out a disagreement. Nobody intervenes.
scene_crew_brawl_close: One side left. They left on their own terms, walking. Nobody ran.
The corner is quiet again. The price of quiet is currently unknown.
Whatever the dispute was, it's been filed.
scene_scaffold_brawl_open: Two workers on the scaffolding job are working something out on the sidewalk.
A contract dispute between two guys and a section of pipe, on {neighborhood}.
The work crew stopped working. Two of them are explaining the situation with their hands.
scene_scaffold_brawl_close: Both workers are back on the job. The scaffolding job remains incomplete either way.
A foreman appeared and the fight ended and everyone pretended it didn't happen.
The fight ended when someone dropped a tool and both of them looked down.
scene_blackout_night_open: The block on {neighborhood} goes dark all at once — one pop, then silence, then generators.
The lights went off from the corner and rolled down the block like dominoes. Grid's down.
Blackout hit {neighborhood}. Windows going dark, candles appearing in the same windows fifteen seconds later.
The streetlights on {neighborhood} killed. The dark came in so fast people looked up.
scene_blackout_day_open: The storefronts lost power on {neighborhood}. Metal gates going down while it's still light.
Every screen on {neighborhood} went blank at the same moment. Grid problem.
The bodega's fridge hum stopped. The whole block stopped. Daytime blackout.
Power died on {neighborhood} in the afternoon. The ice in the carts starts counting down.
scene_blackout_industrial_open: The dock equipment stopped. The whole waterfront block went dark.
Industrial sector lost the grid. Backup generators are the good ones here. Most of them.
The warehouses on {neighborhood} are dark. Emergency lighting, which means amber and not much.
scene_blackout_mid: The dark isn't lifting. People are organizing around it.
Candles in every window now. The street lit from above by other people's emergencies.
The grid is down but the neighborhood isn't. Someone strung an extension cord across the street.
scene_blackout_end: The lights came back. People cheered once and went back inside.
Power restored on {neighborhood}. The reasons are not released.
The grid came up by degrees — first one streetlight, then three, then all of them.
scene_shakedown_open: Faction muscle posted at the shop entrance on {neighborhood}. The owner is making change fast.
Two enforcers at the register of the bodega on {neighborhood}. The line of customers is very patient.
The collectors are here. The shop is open but the atmosphere is not.
A protection stop on {neighborhood} — two people at the door, one inside, everyone aware.
scene_shakedown_mid: The owner is talking slow and nodding. This is going fine, in the way where fine means nothing broke.
The tally is being written. The pen is very deliberate.
One of the enforcers bought something. This is apparently normal.
scene_shakedown_close: The collectors walk out and down the street. The owner locks up for ten minutes.
They left with something in a bag. The shop reopens immediately.
The muscle is gone. The shop owner mops something by the register.
scene_dock_shakedown_open: A dock crew stopped unloading. Faction representatives arrived with a clipboard.
The cargo on the waterfront isn't moving until numbers are agreed on. The sun is moving.
Two enforcers at the warehouse gate on {neighborhood}. The foreman is checking his phone for a number.
scene_dock_shakedown_close: The cargo moves. The dock crew resumes without looking at anyone.
The clipboard people walk back to the water. Everyone goes back to work.
Numbers agreed. The dock foreman doesn't look relieved.
scene_cult_shakedown_open: Cult collectors are asking for tithe at the corner of {neighborhood}. They call it a donation.
Two believers with an envelope are working the block. It isn't optional.
The antenna-cult doing collections on {neighborhood}. The rate is a standard rate. Mostly.
scene_cult_shakedown_mid: The envelope gets full slowly. The collectors don't rush.
A merchant paid with components instead of currency. They took it.
street_beat: Two voices behind a sealed door going fast. You don't hear words, only heat.
A livery cab idles at the corner for eight minutes, then leaves without taking anyone.
A deal in a building doorway. Hands don't show. Nods.
Someone in the phone-less phone booth on {neighborhood} is actually on the phone.
A drone comes in low, clears the scaffolding by two feet, drops a bag on a fire escape.
Glass breaks one floor up. Nobody on the street looks up.
A kid on a bike does one slow lap around the block and stops at the same corner.
Someone crying in the phone-less phone booth. The door is open.
Two people hug in the middle of the sidewalk for a long time. Neither lets go first.
A guy with a shopping cart full of copper moves down the alley at speed.
A dog tied to a parking meter is reading the street better than anyone.
A window shade opens, someone looks out at the street for thirty seconds, closes.
The halal cart on {neighborhood} is out of everything. The sign says 'come back.' It doesn't say when.
A fight you can't see — just the sound and the crowd adjusting away from the mouth of the alley.
A man in a suit standing in front of a rowhouse writing in a notebook. He sees you see him.
Three guys on a stoop all look in the same direction at the same moment, then don't.
A fire escape garden drops a tomato. It lands wrong.
Footsteps above, fast, crossing from one building to the next via the rooftop. Parkour, maybe. Maybe not.
A folded note under a wiper blade. Wind takes it before anyone reads it.
Someone put a couch on the curb. It's gone in forty minutes.
A livery cab has had its hazards on for two hours. The driver is still in it.
An argument dies the moment you pass. Resumes after.
The door of the abandoned storefront on {neighborhood} is ajar. It wasn't yesterday.
A child hands something to an adult from a second-story window on a string. The adult pockets it and walks.
Sirens three blocks south, then more sirens east, then quiet. Something happened.
A rat the size of a shoe crosses the intersection with purpose.
Two men shaking hands for the fourth time in a row, each one more final than the last.
Someone stencils the pavement three feet ahead of you. Gone around the corner before you read it.
ambient: The scaffolding on this block has been up so long moss grew on the planks.
A bodega cat watches you from behind the Red Bull display. It has watched everyone.
The BQE hums in the distance like it always does — traffic or grief, same sound.
A halal cart's grill is still warm. No one's tending it.
Three cones in the street for a reason nobody is investigating.
The fire escape above you has a garden. The garden has a lock.
Someone wrote a phone number on the doorframe in grease pencil. It's been there long enough to fade.
A stoop chair. No one in it.
The building to your left still has scaffolding. The permit expired in 2031.
A news drone passes overhead, slow, making itself very obvious.
The awning overhead reads something in two languages and the English half is illegible.
A pay-laundry bag hanging from a fire escape — someone still waiting for a pickup.
The curb smells like bleach. Recent bleach.
